input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Digital Twin constellation orchestrated through MBSE: functional and logical architectures are defined in SysML, component and connector relations captured in ADLs such as ACME, AADL and MontiArc (including automaton extensions). Behavior is further expressed via state machines in SysML v1.7, Simulink simulation models and software modules written in C++ or Java. These heterogeneous model notations are integrated within the same Digital Twin system, allowing coordination of simulation, code execution and data exchange across model layers.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The system’s computational models are derived using Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE). Requirement, functional, logical and product/technical layers are specified with SysML v1.7 Block Definition diagrams, Internal Block diagrams, and state‑machine elements; at the logical level MontiArc Automaton or SysML state machines encode behavior. Component‑and‑connector relationships are captured in architecture description languages such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc. Geometric models are represented via SysML’s 3‑D extensions and linked to the component model through unique identifiers. Analytical simulation is performed in MathWorks Simulink, and executable models may be coded in C++ or Java, with their outputs streamed into a central database. Data management follows a versioned repository approach, where each model artefact is stored in the MBSE toolset and tied to requirement constraints via SysML requirement bindings, establishing clear traceability across the Digital Twin constellation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Automated one‑way data flow from the physical object’s state to the digital representation; the digital shadow aggregates contextual data traces and their abstractions for a specific purpose as defined in the reference model.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: Development of a Digital Twin follows a multi‑level Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) approach, with explicit separation of abstraction layers: 1) Requirements level, 2) Functional architecture (using SysML architectural diagrams and model elements), 3) Logical architecture (described with ADLs such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc and SysML IBDs), and 4) Product/technical layer (implementation provided in C++, Java, or by simulation in MathWorks Simulink). Transitions between stages are managed by mapping constructs between the modeling notations, while governance of the lifecycle relies on the chosen MBSE methodology (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT) to ensure traceability and consistency.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Engineering of the Digital Twin is based on Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) and utilizes standardized modeling languages. The development workflow incorporates one of the established MBSE methodologies—RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM (Object‑Oriented Systems Engineering), SPES (Synch. Process for Engineering Software) or SMArDT—to structure requirements, functions, and physics models. Architecture descriptions are realized through SysML (v1.7) for logical and functional views, MontiArc (planar block diagrams and automaton extensions) for behavior, and ACME/AADL for component‑connector topology. Implementation layers are modeled in C++ or Java or in simulation tools such as MathWorks Simulink. Version control, quality assurance, testing and validation approaches, and explicit evolution management are not detailed in the supplied material.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin leverages a suite of industry standards for modeling and description, including SysML (ISO/IEC 15929‑based), MontiArc (description of behavior and automata), ACME and AADL (component‑connector Architectural Description Languages). No explicit mention of compliance frameworks, certification requirements or interoperability standards is included in the provided material.
extracted_characteristics.data_ownership_and_privacy: The Asset Administration Shell (AAS) schema as described in the semantic AAS reference (Bader & Maleshkova 46, Wei et al. 47, Platform I 4.0 50) attaches each digital twin to a logical owner via an ‘owner’ property expressed as a URN that identifies the legal entity responsible for the asset. Data privacy is encoded in the AAS’s Custom Properties section; each property can be marked with a privacy level (e.g., ‘public’, ‘restricted’, ‘confidential’) which is referenced by the IoT platform (Thingsboard 53) when enforcing access. The AAS also contains a ‘businessDocument’ property that holds signed data‑sharing agreements (encoded as JSON Web Signatures). Data sharing and consent are managed at run time by the MQTT broker Mosquitto (52), which requires TLS/SSL encryption and username/password authentication; access control lists are evaluated against the owner and privacy annotations stored in the AAS.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: Security follows the hierarchy of the RAMI 4.0 reference architecture (Frysak et al. 48, D.D.I. 49).  All device communications are performed over MQTT using Mosquitto, which provides role‑based authentication, TLS 1.3 encryption, and broker‑side ACLs that enforce device identity and permission checks.  Thingsboard (53) supplements this with JWT‑based authentication, OAuth2 support, role‑based access control, and audit logging of every data transaction.  Safety‑critical operations are protected by fail‑safe mechanisms defined in the RAMI 4.0 Safety layer: high‑priority CAN messages deliver emergency stop commands, watchdog timers reset the control daemon on failure, and redundant control firmware implements ISO IEC 61508‑A9 safety integrity level requirements.  The model‑driven engineering method (Heithoff et al. 43) generates simulation models that are validated against IEC 61508 and IEC 61511 safety cases to certify the twin’s control logic before deployment.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.
